global_constraints.G1: pass (with caveat) — No authors, arxiv IDs, or the restricted-identifier strings appear (verified by grep over author-style names and ID patterns). The named result 'intercept theorem' appears once on line 458 (in §5.3 informal forward-direction sketch). This is a named classical theorem rather than a cited author. Strictly interpreted, G1 forbids naming classical theorems; the document is borderline here but does not name the reduction's author or paper.
global_constraints.G2: pass — every lemma is constructed and proved in §4 with an explicit construction; every field-theoretic step in §5 is derived; §6 depends only on §4's lemmas plus §6.4. One notable exception: §5.3 reverse-containment proof is explicitly labeled 'proof sketch' (multiplication/division/sqrt via intercept-theorem and altitude-in-semicircle are named but not formally derived). The compass-only versions of these are given in §6.3 and are self-contained.
global_constraints.G3: pass — section cross-references resolve (§6.3 ↔ §6.4, §10 examples match Lemma 4.1/4.4, etc.). Headline 'T = {C}' consistent with §8's declaration. Minor inconsistency: in §10.1 step 3, X_3 = (3r/2, r√3/2) uses a different coordinate frame from Lemma 4.2 where X_3 = (-r/2, r√3/2); both are internally correct under their respective frames (verified by recomputation), but the same label for different points is mildly confusing.
global_constraints.G4: fail on (d); pass on (a), (b), (c), (e). (a) Document claims T = {C} is minimal universally sufficient — consistent with §8 declaration and §6.6 theorem. (b) §7 proves {L} insufficient via the S = {A, B} counterexample: only line(A, B) is producible, no new points; midpoint of AB is {L, C}-constructible. The 'strengthening' is correctly cautious (explicitly acknowledges the 'given circle with center' case in §11.5). (c) §5.2 forward containment: line-line in K_S (linear system), line-circle in K_S[√α] ⊆ K_S (one square root adjunction), circle-circle reduces to line-circle (radical axis). Each step stays in K_S after ≤1 square root adjunction. Sound. (d) CIRCULARITY in §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ: §6.3's real-square-root step 8 constructs circle(O, i√a) and invokes §6.4 to intersect with real axis. Here P=O ∈ ℓ and Q=i√a ∉ ℓ. §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ explicitly admits it cannot be discharged compass-only and defers to §6.3's real-axis machinery — but §6.3 is what we are in the middle of constructing. The document's parenthetical 'alternative' also concedes it 'degenerates and yields no new intersection compass-only without invoking §6.3's machinery.' This is a material gap in the sufficiency proof. (e) §10 examples: §10.1 midpoint — recomputed: A=(0,0), B=(r,0), K_1∩K_2 yields X_1=(r/2, r√3/2); K_1∩K_3 yields A and X_3=(3r/2, r√3/2); K_1∩K_4 yields X_1 and X_5=(2r,0); inversion of (2r,0) in circle(A,r) gives (r/2, 0) = (A+B)/2. ✓ Only C-primitive. §10.2 foot of perpendicular — uses Lemma 4.1 (reflect P via J_1, J_2 intersection) + Lemma 4.4 midpoint. Only C. Coordinate verification P=(p,q) → P'=(p,-q) → foot = (p,0). ✓ §10.3 reflection — just Lemma 4.1, 2 compass circles, verified by equal-distance argument. ✓

priority_issues.section: §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ and §6.3 real-square-root step 8
priority_issues.issue: Circular dependency: §6.3's real-square-root construction step 8 needs to intersect circle(O, i√a) with the real axis. The center O lies on the real axis and Q = i√a does not. §6.4 Case 2 handles this only by invoking §6.3's real-axis machinery (translation and scaling) — but §6.3's scaling is multiplication via 'invert in circle of radius √m_1', which itself requires the square-root construction we are trying to build. The parenthetical alternative in §6.4 concedes 'this degenerates and yields no new intersection compass-only without invoking §6.3's machinery.' As written, the sufficiency proof has a gap at exactly the square-root step. The headline answer {C} is still correct (it is a standard result) but the argument does not close.
priority_issues.suggestion: Add an explicit compass-only line-circle intersection for Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ that does not depend on multiplication or square-root. One clean route: use Lemma 4.6 (parallelogram completion) to translate Q along ℓ. Given P ∈ ℓ, Q ∉ ℓ, and any R ∈ ℓ with R ≠ P (e.g., one of the line-defining points A, B), construct the translate T = Q + (R − P) via parallelogram completion — this gives a point with the same perpendicular offset as Q but shifted along ℓ. Then build an auxiliary circle centered at T whose compass-only intersection with a symmetric partner circle reveals a point on ℓ at distance |PQ| from P. Whatever the specific sub-routine, it must be free-standing and not route through §6.3.

priority_issues.issue: Reverse containment proof is only a sketch. Addition, multiplication, division, and square root constructions are described but not rigorously derived from {L, C} primitives; 'intercept theorem' is named (minor G1 concern) and 'altitude construction' is named without derivation.
priority_issues.suggestion: Either spell out each field-operation construction step by step (similar to §6.3's detail level), or rename 'intercept theorem' to 'similar-triangles proportionality' and state the derivation in one paragraph. This section is logically important (it establishes the forward half of the field characterization used in §6) and should be at the same rigor level as §6.3.

priority_issues.issue: The 'sequence of progressively smaller sufficient primitive subsets' (C3) is declared in §3 as a procedure but not executed as a walk-down. The document jumps from {L, C} directly to {C}. It argues {L} is insufficient and {C} is sufficient, but does not walk through intermediate candidates or discuss why one cannot try a weaker line primitive. §8 is a one-paragraph declaration, not a walk-down.
priority_issues.suggestion: Insert between §3 and §5 a short narrative: candidate subsets of {L, C} are ∅, {L}, {C}, {L, C}. Rule out ∅ (trivially reaches only S). Rule out {L} (preview of §7). Leaves {C} as the unique candidate that could be both proper subset and sufficient. Making this 4-bullet elimination explicit satisfies the 'progressively smaller' rubric without padding.

priority_issues.issue: Label X_3 is reused with different coordinates in Lemma 4.2 vs §10.1 (different coordinate frames). Mildly confusing but both computations are internally correct.
priority_issues.suggestion: Rename §10.1's new point (e.g., Y_3 or W_3) to avoid collision.

priority_issues.issue: 'Intercept theorem' is a named classical theorem. Strict reading of G1 forbids naming classical theorems.
priority_issues.suggestion: Replace 'the intercept theorem (similar triangles cut by parallel lines)' with just 'similar triangles cut by parallel lines'.

priority_issues.issue: Case 2 of inversion requires iterating Lemma 4.2 doubling AND then halving via Lemma 4.4, but Lemma 4.4 (midpoint) itself uses Lemma 4.3 — introducing a potential circular dependency. The text does note this should use 'halving' but does not verify that halving can be done without Lemma 4.3 Case 2.
priority_issues.suggestion: Check and state: the halving step uses Lemma 4.4, which uses Lemma 4.3 Case 1 only (since |AB'| = 2r ≥ r/2 by the doubling that precedes it). Document this chain to dispel the apparent loop.

priority_issues.issue: Multiplication uses 'inversion in circle of radius √m_1'. Constructing that circle requires the real-square-root construction, which requires multiplication (via the altitude construction: (1+a)/2 involves division by 2, which is midpoint, OK; but subsequent steps chain back to line-circle intersection). The dependency order between multiplication, square-root, and line-circle intersection is not cleanly spelled out.
priority_issues.suggestion: Add a dependency diagram or explicit topological order: addition ← antipode ← circle-circle; multiplication ← inversion + square-root; square-root ← altitude + line-circle-intersection; line-circle-intersection ← Lemma 4.1 + circle-circle (Case 1) or free-standing fix for Case 2. Make clear whether Case 2 is ever actually needed in §6.3 — it is (step 8), which is where the circularity arises.

strengths: Clear framing (§2) with explicit primitives, construction semantics, simulation/equivalence definitions, and point-vs-curve scope.
§4 compass-only toolkit: six lemmas with constructions; Lemma 4.2 has a complete algebraic verification; Lemma 4.3 has a complete algebraic verification. Recomputations check out.
§5.2 forward containment proof is rigorous — handles line-line, line-circle, circle-circle cases cleanly; each stays in K_S after ≤ 1 square-root adjunction.
§6.5 explicitly handles the line-line intersection 'objection' via the algebraic bypass — this is a key move and is argued cleanly.

§10's three worked examples are verifiable: §10.1 has a coordinate-level verification recomputed by hand and checks out; §10.3 is a straightforward two-circle intersection; §10.2 layers §10.3 + Lemma 4.4.

§11 surfaces 8 non-trivial open questions (complexity cost, non-Euclidean, rusty compass, 3-ary circle primitive, degenerate S, smaller-than-C primitives, degrees of freedom, empty S). Generous and appropriate.
Motivation §1 cites five analogous phenomena from first principles without naming any author (Boolean completeness, combinators, OISC, group generators, 'hypothetical geometric precedent').
overall_assessment: The document delivers the correct headline answer (T = {C}) and develops the correct overall structure: field-theoretic characterization, forward containment, reverse containment via compass-only realization of field generators. It is self-contained, avoids naming papers/authors, and its three worked examples are genuinely compass-only and verify correctly. The principal flaw is a circularity in §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ that undermines §6.3's real-square-root step 8 — the proof chain is not technically closed at this joint, though the gap is patchable and the answer is classically correct. Secondary issues: §5.3 reverse containment is a sketch rather than a rigorous derivation, and §3's 'procedure' is not actually walked through as a sequence of shrinking candidates (§8 is a one-paragraph declaration).
